subject: CS-CV
description: Initial project proposal. The goal is to investigate methods for deploying lightweight object detection models on resource-constrained edge devices, aiming for real-time performance (e.g., >15 FPS). A high-level plan is drafted to explore model quantization and pruning techniques.
reason: This is the inaugural event of the research project. It is essential to first define the core research questions, establish clear goals, and create a high-level plan to guide subsequent work. This proposal sets the foundation for the entire project.

subject: CS-CV
description: Based on the initial proposal, a detailed methodological scheme is designed. We select a baseline model (YOLOv5-nano), a target edge device (NVIDIA Jetson Nano), and a benchmark dataset (COCO 2017). The scheme outlines the specific model compression techniques to be evaluated: 8-bit post-training quantization and structured pruning.
reason: Following the high-level proposal (e_01), the next logical step is to translate the abstract goals and plan into a concrete experimental design. This scheme design (e_02) specifies the exact models, hardware, and techniques to be used, which is necessary before any experiments can be run.

subject: CS-CV
description: A pilot experiment is conducted to establish a baseline. The un-optimized YOLOv5-nano model is deployed on the Jetson Nano, and its performance (mAP on a small subset of COCO validation, and inference speed) is measured. This initial run helps verify the experimental toolchain and provides a baseline for comparison with compressed models.
reason: With the experimental scheme (e_02) defined, a pilot study is the logical next step to validate the setup and gather baseline performance data. These initial results are crucial for evaluating the effectiveness of the compression techniques planned for the main experiments and ensuring the project's feasibility.

subject: CS-CV
description: The baseline results from the pilot experiment are analyzed. The analysis confirms the un-optimized model runs at ~10 FPS on the Jetson Nano, which is below the real-time goal of >15 FPS, formally justifying the investigation into compression techniques. The baseline mAP is established as the quality benchmark to maintain after optimization.
reason: After gathering baseline performance data in the pilot experiment (e_03), a formal analysis is the logical next step. This event evaluates the baseline against the initial research goals (e_01), confirms that the proposed optimization work is necessary, and sets a clear performance target for the main experiments.

subject: CS-CV
description: The first main experiment is conducted, focusing on post-training quantization. The baseline YOLOv5-nano model is quantized to 8-bit integers (INT8) and deployed on the Jetson Nano. Its performance (mAP and FPS) is then measured on the same COCO subset used for the baseline.
reason: After the analysis in e_04 confirmed the baseline model's performance is insufficient, the next logical step is to execute the first optimization strategy outlined in the method scheme (e_02). This main experiment applies quantization to generate the first set of results for an optimized model, directly addressing the core research goals.

subject: CS-CV
description: The performance of the INT8 quantized model from the main experiment is analyzed. The results show a significant speed-up to ~18 FPS, meeting the real-time goal. However, the analysis also reveals a noticeable drop in mean Average Precision (mAP) compared to the un-optimized baseline, highlighting a classic speed-accuracy trade-off.
reason: After running the first main experiment (e_05), it is crucial to analyze the results to understand the impact of quantization. This analysis compares the new performance against the project's goals (e_01) and the baseline, revealing both the success in meeting the speed target and the new challenge of accuracy degradation, which will guide the next research steps.

subject: CS-CV
description: To address the accuracy drop observed with post-training quantization, the method scheme is revised. The team decides to implement Quantization-Aware Training (QAT), a technique that fine-tunes the model with simulated quantization to recover accuracy. This new approach will be compared against both the baseline and the previous INT8 model.
reason: The analysis in e_06 revealed a significant accuracy loss from post-training quantization. It is a logical next step to refine the experimental method to specifically target this issue. By updating the scheme to include Quantization-Aware Training, the project is directly attempting to solve a problem identified in the previous experiment, aiming for a better speed-accuracy trade-off.

subject: CS-CV
description: Following the revised scheme, a new main experiment is conducted using Quantization-Aware Training (QAT). The YOLOv5-nano model is fine-tuned on the COCO training set with simulated quantization, then converted to an INT8 model and deployed on the Jetson Nano. Performance metrics (mAP and FPS) are collected for this new model.
reason: After revising the methodology in e_07 to include Quantization-Aware Training, the immediate next step is to execute this new experimental plan. This experiment directly addresses the accuracy degradation problem identified in the analysis of the previous model (e_06), aiming to produce a model that meets both the speed and accuracy goals.

subject: CS-CV
description: The results of the Quantization-Aware Training (QAT) experiment are analyzed. The QAT model achieves ~17.5 FPS, maintaining real-time performance, and successfully recovers most of the accuracy lost during post-training quantization. Its mAP is now only slightly lower than the original un-optimized baseline, representing a much better speed-accuracy trade-off.
reason: Following the main experiment with QAT (e_08), a detailed analysis is necessary to evaluate its effectiveness. This step compares the new results against the baseline and previous PTQ model results (from e_06), confirming that the revised scheme (e_07) successfully addressed the accuracy degradation problem while still meeting the project's performance goals.

subject: CS-CV
description: With strong results from the Quantization-Aware Training (QAT) experiments, the team begins to formally write up the findings. This effort focuses on drafting the 'Methodology' and 'Results' sections of the paper, describing the baseline, post-training quantization, and QAT approaches, and presenting the comparative performance tables for speed and accuracy.
reason: After the successful analysis of the QAT model in e_09, it is a prudent research practice to document these significant findings. Drafting the paper now ensures the key results and methods are captured accurately while they are fresh, and it helps build the narrative for the final publication before embarking on the next set of experiments on model pruning.

subject: CS-CV
description: The team shifts focus to the second optimization technique from the original plan: structured pruning. An experiment is conducted to prune 50% of the channels from the baseline YOLOv5-nano model. The pruned model is then fine-tuned and its performance (mAP and FPS) is evaluated on the Jetson Nano.
reason: With the quantization investigation yielding strong results (e_09) and a draft written (e_10), the project now proceeds to the next phase outlined in the original research plan (e_01). This experiment on structured pruning explores a different optimization axis, aiming to provide a comprehensive comparison of compression techniques.

subject: CS-CV
description: The results from the structured pruning experiment are analyzed. The pruned model shows a significant speed increase, reaching ~22 FPS, the fastest yet. However, this comes at the cost of a severe drop in mAP, even worse than the initial post-training quantization attempt, making it unsuitable for applications where accuracy is critical.
reason: After conducting the main experiment on structured pruning (e_11), it is essential to analyze the performance data. This analysis evaluates the speed-accuracy trade-off of pruning and compares it against the project goals and the results from the previous quantization experiments (e_09), providing a clear picture of this technique's limitations.

subject: CS-CV
description: Inspired by the high speed of the pruned model but poor accuracy (e_12), and the balanced performance of the QAT model (e_09), the team revises the research plan to explore hybrid methods. A new scheme is designed to first apply a less aggressive structured pruning (e.g., 30% sparsity) and then use Quantization-Aware Training on the pruned model to recover accuracy.
reason: The analysis in e_12 showed that simple pruning is too detrimental to accuracy. Instead of abandoning pruning, a more sophisticated, hybrid approach is a logical next step. This new scheme combines the two techniques investigated so far (pruning and quantization) to see if their strengths can be combined to achieve a superior speed-accuracy trade-off, representing a natural evolution of the project's plan.

subject: CS-CV
description: A main experiment is run to test the new hybrid optimization scheme. First, the baseline model is pruned with 30% sparsity. Then, Quantization-Aware Training (QAT) is applied to the pruned model to recover accuracy. The final hybrid model is deployed and benchmarked on the Jetson Nano.
reason: Following the design of the hybrid pruning-quantization scheme in e_13, the logical next step is to execute this experiment. This test aims to synthesize the findings from previous experiments by combining the speed gains from pruning (e_12) with the accuracy recovery of QAT (e_09) to achieve a potentially superior result.

subject: CS-CV
description: The performance of the hybrid model (30% pruning + QAT) is analyzed. The results are highly positive: the model achieves ~20 FPS, faster than the QAT-only model, while maintaining an mAP very close to the QAT model's performance. This hybrid approach is identified as the best-performing method so far, offering a superior speed-accuracy trade-off.
reason: After generating results for the hybrid model in e_14, a thorough analysis is the critical next step. This event evaluates whether the combination of pruning and QAT successfully synthesized their respective benefits, comparing the new results against all prior experiments (e.g., e_09, e_12) to determine the most effective optimization strategy for the project.

subject: CS-CV
description: With the hybrid model identified as the most successful approach, the team focuses on creating a complete draft of the research paper. This involves integrating the results and analysis from the pruning and hybrid experiments into the existing document, and writing the abstract, introduction, and conclusion to highlight the final, superior speed-accuracy trade-off achieved.
reason: After the analysis in e_15 confirmed the superiority of the hybrid model, the project has a conclusive result and a clear narrative. The next logical step is to consolidate all findings into a full paper draft, which is a critical step towards disseminating the research and completing the project.

subject: CS-CV
description: The complete paper draft undergoes internal review and refinement. Key visualizations, such as a scatter plot of mAP vs. FPS for all evaluated models, are created to clearly illustrate the speed-accuracy trade-offs. The manuscript is edited for clarity, flow, and impact, preparing it for submission.
reason: After producing a complete draft in e_16, the next step in a realistic research workflow is to refine the manuscript and generate figures for publication. This event represents the crucial polishing phase that turns a draft with all the necessary components into a compelling, submission-ready paper by improving the narrative and visually summarizing the key findings.

subject: CS-CV
description: The paper manuscript is finalized and submitted to a top-tier computer vision conference (e.g., CVPR). The project is concluded, having successfully identified and validated a hybrid optimization technique that achieves a superior speed-accuracy trade-off for real-time object detection on edge devices.
reason: The project has reached a successful conclusion. A clear research narrative has been established, from initial exploration to identifying a novel hybrid approach (e_15) that outperforms other methods. The findings have been consolidated into a submission-ready manuscript (e_17), making submission and project conclusion the appropriate final step.
